nativeRange.habitat: Choat's Leopard Wrasse is a subtropical reef-associated species found along channels and seaward reefs of the eastern Australian coast, including the southern Great Barrier Reef, Middleton and Elizabeth Reefs, and northern New South Wales, as well as in New Caledonian waters. It is typically encountered at depths from the surface down to around 30 m.

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (≥7.5 cm) for nocturnal burying
well-established live rock with abundant copepod and amphipod populations

diet.notes: Strongly tied to live microfauna — has been documented foraging on copepods, amphipods, foraminiferans, and other small hard-shelled invertebrates picked from sand and live rock. In captivity, generally advised to be introduced only into well-matured systems (commonly cited as 12–18 months or older) with abundant pod populations and ideally a connected refugium. Over time most specimens can be weaned onto vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp, and other small meaty foods, and some will eventually take high-quality marine flake or pellet; many keepers report that prepared foods alone do not sustain this species long-term without ongoing pod replenishment.

avoidWith: tangs, angelfish, and hawkfish added before the wrasse has settled

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by amfstocker; 891x522. Full lateral view of a live in-colour individual showing the diagnostic strong red-orange spotting/blotching on a pearl-white ground with forked red-margined tail; slightly soft but the best diagnostic full-body shot.
Photo by David Witherall; 640x640. Different individual in the leopard-spotted phase (orange face, dark red-orange spots on white); adds variety, lower resolution.
Photo by amfstocker; 716x552. Second angle of the same red-spotted individual as the recommended pick, showing head detail and caudal fin; near-duplicate kept as extra.

summary: An iconic Australian collector wrasse with a pearl-white body overlaid by red-orange spotting; widely considered expert-only owing to high shipping mortality, copper sensitivity, and dependence on copious live microfauna.
careNotes: Choat's Leopard Wrasse is one of the most sought-after Macropharyngodon species and is widely regarded as expert-only. Wild specimens are collected from Australian and New Caledonian reefs and are typically imported in small numbers, commanding a high price and arriving in variable condition. Long-term success is generally tied to a mature, microfauna-rich reef system (commonly cited as 12–18 months old or more) with abundant copepod and amphipod populations, ideally backed by a connected refugium; new tanks are widely reported to result in slow starvation. A deep, fine sand bed (commonly cited at around 7.5 cm or more) is generally considered essential, as individuals sleep buried at night and dive into the sand when startled. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised — Macropharyngodon wrasses have been known to launch from open tanks when spooked. Within the genus, M. choati is the species specifically noted as copper sensitive — documented as the exception to standard medicated quarantine protocols, kept in copper-free quarantine systems rather than the typical copper treatment used for other wrasses. Copper-based treatments should be avoided. Tends to settle best when introduced before potentially harassing tankmates such as tangs, dwarf angels, hawkfish, or damsels. Most aquarists keep this species singly or as a confirmed mated pair; while leopard wrasses are protogynous and form harems on natural reefs, multiple Macropharyngodon — including conspecific males — are not generally advised in standard home aquaria. Widely traded as 'Choat's Red Leopard Wrasse' or simply 'Red Leopard Wrasse' due to the strong red-orange spotting on a white ground.
breedingNotes: The species is oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding, typical of the family Labridae. Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby literature reviewed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae / Eupercaria, Randall 1978), distribution (Western Pacific: Australia, 17°S–30°S), depth (usually 0–30 m), preferred temperature (23–26 °C), maximum size (7.6 cm SL — lower than aquarium-trade citations of ~10 cm; spread captured in adultSizeCm range), reproductive mode (oviparous, distinct pairing), and IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: Fishkeeper (Maidenhead Aquatics)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~10 cm / 3.9 in), temperature (24–27 °C — averaged with LiveAquaria 72–78 °F ≈ 22.2–25.6 °C and FishBase 23–26 °C to 23–26 °C here), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.020–1.025, recommended sand-bed depth (7.5 cm / 3 in), jumping/escape behaviour requiring tight-fitting cover-slides, peaceful temperament with caveats around conspecifics and other Macropharyngodon, and warnings against new-tank placement. Lists trade names Australian Leopard Wrasse and Red Leopard Wrasse.

additional.name: LiveAquaria Species Spotlight: Leopard Wrasses of the Genus Macropharyngodon
additional.notes: Genus article that specifically singles out Choat's Leopard Wrasse as the documented copper-sensitive Macropharyngodon, kept in copper-free quarantine systems rather than the standard medicated protocol. Genus-wide confirmation of sand-bed burying, jumping/escape behaviour, and best introduction order relative to potentially aggressive tankmates.

additional.name: UltraMarine Magazine — Species Spotlight: Macropharyngodon choati
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~7.5–7.6 cm), range (Eastern Australia and New Caledonia, including the southern Great Barrier Reef), depth (usually 0–30 m), and trade names (Choat's Wrasse, Red Leopard Wrasse, Red Flecked Wrasse). Notes the species is infrequently available and difficult to obtain in good condition.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby literature reviewed. The species is oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding. As with other Macropharyngodon, individuals are protogynous hermaphrodites — meaning most begin life as females, with the dominant fish in a group eventually transitioning to become a terminal-phase male.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Iconic but demanding Australian collector wrasse: high shipping mortality, copper-sensitive (cannot be treated with standard copper protocols), obligate dependence on copious live copepod/amphipod microfauna from a long-established refugium-supported reef, refuses prepared foods initially, and requires a deep fine sand bed for nocturnal burying. Not appropriate for new or undersized reef systems.
